figures.caption: Fig. 10: Additional editing results on RE10K [56].
Fig. 11: Additional editing results on ScanNet [16]

Fig. 7: Failure case Our method occasionally produces inconsistent edits across views. (Top) When editing the faucet color, the faucet visible in the mirror remains unedited, indicating that the model fails to propagate the edit to reflected regions. (Bottom) When transforming wooden floors into marble, the generated marble texture exhibits inconsistency across views, leading to noticeable appearance variation under different viewpoints.

Fig. 3: Qualitative comparison on DTU [1] and RE10K [56]. We compare FluSplat with DGE [11], EditSplat [26], and ViP3DE [10] under diverse text-guided editing instructions, on DTU(top 3 rows, zero-shot) and RE10K(bottom 2 rows, in-domain). For each method, the large image and its inset correspond to two distinct novel views of the edited 3D scene. Our method produces more consistent color transformations, better structural preservation, fewer cross-view artifacts, and more consistent object removal than prior approaches. In contrast, prior methods exhibit color bleeding, or incomplete edits.

Fig. 8: Qualitative comparison on DTU [1]. We compare FluSplat with DGE [11], EditSplat [26], and ViP3DE [10] under diverse text-guided editing instructions). For each method, the large image and its inset correspond to two distinct novel views of the edited 3D scene. Our method produces more consistent color transformations, better structural preservation, fewer cross-view artifacts, and more consistent object removal than prior approaches. In contrast, prior methods exhibit color bleeding, or incomplete edits.

Fig. 5: Qualitative comparison of cross-view regularization. Without cross-view regularization, edited views may exhibit inconsistent results: either the edit appears in only one view, or both views are edited but with mismatched appearance. Global Diffusion Feature Loss (GDFL) encourages global semantic alignment so that edits are consistently applied across views, while Local Editing Feature Loss (LEFL) further enhances region-level coherence, ensuring consistent appearance within the localized edited areas.

Fig. 4: Cross-view 2D editing comparison on IN2N [19]. Qualitative results comparing prior methods and our model. Our approach produces cross-view edits that better align with the textual instruction.

Fig. 9: Qualitative comparison on RE10K [56]. We compare FluSplat with DGE [11], EditSplat [26], and ViP3DE [10]. For each method, the large image and inset show two different novel views. Our method achieves more consistent object removal and fewer cross-view artifacts than prior approaches.
Table 6: Training configuration and hyperparameters used in our method.

Fig. 2: Cross-view consistent FLUX fine-tuning. A LoRA-adapted FLUX model edits two sparse views while enforcing cross-view coherence. Global Diffusion Feature Loss (GDFL) aligns intermediate diffusion features for global consistency, and Local Editing Feature Loss (LEFL) aligns CLIP-localized regions with DINOv3 features for region-level alignment. Together, these regularizations stabilize single-step editing.
